This article is about the group of Indo-European peoples. For the inhabitants of the modern country of Iran, see [Ethnicities in Iran](/wiki/Ethnicities_in_Iran "Ethnicities in Iran"). For other uses, see [Demographics of Iran](/wiki/Demographics_of_Iran "Demographics of Iran").

"Iranics" redirects here. For the left-leaning italics, see [Italic type § Iranic font style](/wiki/Italic_type#Iranic_font_style "Italic type").

**Iranian peoples**,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrye2004-1) or **Iranic peoples**,[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Schierbrand1922306-2) are a diverse [ethnolinguistic group](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group")[[3]](#cite_note-3) who are identified chiefly by their native usage of any of the [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages"), which are a branch of the [Indo-Iranian languages](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages") within the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages").

The [Proto-Iranians](/wiki/Proto-Iranian_language "Proto-Iranian language") are believed to have emerged as a separate branch of the [Indo-Iranians](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") around the mid-2nd millennium BC.[[4]](#cite_note-Beckwith58-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Mallory308-5) At their peak of expansion in the mid-1st millennium BC, the territory of the Iranian peoples stretched across the entire [Eurasian Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe "Eurasian Steppe"); from the [Danubian Plains](/wiki/Danube "Danube") in the west to the [Ordos Plateau](/wiki/Ordos_Plateau "Ordos Plateau") in the east and the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau") in the south.[[6]](#cite_note-Harmatta348-6)

The [ancient Iranian peoples](/wiki/List_of_ancient_Iranian_peoples "List of ancient Iranian peoples") who emerged after the 1st millennium BC include the [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans"), the [Bactrians](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria"), the [Dahae](/wiki/Dahae "Dahae"), the [Khwarazmians](/wiki/Khwarazm "Khwarazm"), the [Massagetae](/wiki/Massagetae "Massagetae"), the [Medes](/wiki/Medes "Medes"), the [Parthians](/wiki/Parthia "Parthia"), the [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"), the [Sagartians](/wiki/Sagartians "Sagartians"), the [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka"), the [Sarmatians](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians"), the [Scythians](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians"), the [Sogdians](/wiki/Sogdia "Sogdia"), and likely the [Cimmerians](/wiki/Cimmerians "Cimmerians"), among other Iranian-speaking peoples of [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"), Central Asia, [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), and the [Eastern Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe#Mongolian-Manchurian_Steppe_(Eastern_Steppe) "Eurasian Steppe").

In the 1st millennium AD, their area of settlement, which was mainly concentrated in the steppes and deserts of [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) was significantly reduced due to the expansion of the [Slavic peoples](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"), the [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples"), the [Turkic peoples](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples"), the [Uralic peoples](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages"), the [Mongolic peoples](/wiki/Mongolic_peoples "Mongolic peoples"), and the [Caucasian peoples](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_Caucasus "Ethnic groups in the Caucasus"); many were subjected to [Slavicization](/wiki/Slavicisation "Slavicisation")[[8]](#cite_note-The_Sarmatians,_600_BC-AD_450-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Taylor_&_Francis-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Women_in_Russia-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Slovene_Studies-11) and [Turkification](/wiki/Turkification "Turkification").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Modern Iranian peoples include the [Baloch](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people"), the [Gilaks](/wiki/Gilaks "Gilaks"), the [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds"), the [Lurs](/wiki/Lurs "Lurs"), the [Mazanderanis](/wiki/Mazanderani_people "Mazanderani people"), the [Ossetians](/wiki/Ossetians "Ossetians"), the [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), the [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"), the [Tats](/wiki/Tat_people_(Caucasus) "Tat people (Caucasus)"), the [Tajiks](/wiki/Tajiks "Tajiks"), the [Talysh](/wiki/Talysh_people "Talysh people"), the [Zazas](/wiki/Zazas "Zazas")[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) and the [Wakhis](/wiki/Wakhi_people "Wakhi people"). Their current distribution spreads across the Iranian Plateau – stretching from the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") in the north to the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") in the south and from [eastern Anatolia](/wiki/Eastern_Anatolia_Region "Eastern Anatolia Region") in the west to [western Xinjiang](/wiki/Tashkurgan_Tajik_Autonomous_County "Tashkurgan Tajik Autonomous County") in the east – covering a region that is sometimes called [Greater Iran](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran"), representing the extent of the Iranian-speaking peoples and the reach of their geopolitical and cultural influence.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Name

See also: [Arya (Iran)](/wiki/Arya_(Iran) "Arya (Iran)") and [Iran (word)](/wiki/Iran_(word) "Iran (word)")

The term *[Iran](/wiki/Iran_(word) "Iran (word)")* derives directly from [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian") *Ērān* (𐭠𐭩𐭥𐭠𐭭‎) and [Parthian](/wiki/Parthian_language "Parthian language") *Aryān* (𐭀𐭓𐭉𐭀𐭍‎).[[17]](#cite_note-MacKenzie-17) The [Middle Iranian](/wiki/Middle_Iranian_languages "Middle Iranian languages") terms *ērān* and *aryān* are oblique plural forms of [gentilic](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") *ēr-* (in Middle Persian) and *ary-* (in Parthian), both deriving from [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian") *ariya-* (𐎠𐎼𐎡𐎹), [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") *airiia-* (𐬀𐬌𐬭𐬌𐬌𐬀) and [Proto-Iranian](/wiki/Proto-Iranian_language "Proto-Iranian language") *\*arya-*.[[17]](#cite_note-MacKenzie-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Schmitt_Aryans1-18)

There have been many attempts to qualify the verbal root of *ar-* in Old Iranian *arya-*. The following are according to 1957 and later linguists:

* Emmanuel Laroche (1957): [*ar**a**-* "to fit"](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/h%E2%82%82er- "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/h₂er-") ("fitting", "proper").  
  Old Iranian *arya-* being descended from [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") \**ar-yo-*, meaning "(skillfully) assembler".[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* Georges Dumézil (1958): *ar-* "to share" (as a union).
* Harold Walter Bailey (1959): *ar-* "to beget" ("born", "nurturing").
* Émil Benveniste (1969): [*ar-* "to fit"](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/h%E2%82%82er- "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/h₂er-") ("companionable").

Unlike the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") *ārya-* (*[Aryan](/wiki/Aryan "Aryan")*), the Old Iranian term has solely an ethnic meaning.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21) Today, the Old Iranian *arya-* remains in ethno-linguistic names such as *Iran*, *[Alan](/wiki/Alans "Alans")*, *[Ir](/wiki/Ossetians "Ossetians")*, and *[Iron](/wiki/Iron_dialect "Iron dialect")*.[[22]](#cite_note-Bailey-22)[[17]](#cite_note-MacKenzie-17)[[23]](#cite_note-Dalby,_Andrew_2004-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Darius_I_the_Great%27s_inscription.jpg/250px-Darius_I_the_Great%27s_inscription.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darius_I_the_Great%27s_inscription.jpg)

The [Bistun Inscription](/wiki/Behistun_Inscription "Behistun Inscription") of [Darius the Great](/wiki/Darius_I "Darius I") describes itself to have been composed in *Arya* [language or script].

In the [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages"), the gentilic is attested as a self-identifier included in ancient inscriptions and the literature of [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta").[[25]](#cite_note-Bailey_Arya-25)[[a]](#cite_note-26) The earliest [epigraphically](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") attested reference to the word *arya-* occurs in the [Bistun Inscription](/wiki/Behistun_Inscription "Behistun Inscription") of the 6th century BC. The inscription of Bistun (or *Behistun*; [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian_language "Old Persian language"): *Bagastana*) describes itself to have been composed in *Arya* [language or script]. As is also the case for all other Old Iranian language usage, the *arya* of the inscription does not signify anything but *Iranian*.[[26]](#cite_note-Gershevitch-27)

In royal Old Persian inscriptions, the term *arya-* appears in three different contexts:[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Bailey-22)

* As the name of the language of the Old Persian version of the inscription of [Darius I](/wiki/Darius_I "Darius I") in the Bistun Inscription.
* As the ethnic background of Darius the Great in inscriptions at [Rustam Relief](/wiki/Naqsh-e_Rustam "Naqsh-e Rustam") and Susa (Dna, Dse) and the ethnic background of [Xerxes I](/wiki/Xerxes_I "Xerxes I") in the inscription from Persepolis (Xph).
* As the definition of the God of Iranians, [Ohrmazd](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda"), in the [Elamite](/wiki/Elamite_language "Elamite language") version of the Bistun Inscription.

In the Dna and Dse, Darius and Xerxes describe themselves as "an Achaemenid, a Persian, son of a Persian, and an Aryan, of Aryan stock".[[27]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-28) Although Darius the Great called his language *arya-* ("Iranian"),[[27]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-28) modern scholars refer to it as *Old Persian*[[27]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-28) because it is the ancestor of the modern Persian language.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The [trilingual](/wiki/Trilingual "Trilingual") inscription erected by the command of [Shapur I](/wiki/Shapur_I "Shapur I") gives a more clear description. The languages used are Parthian, Middle Persian, and Greek. In Greek inscription says *"ego ... tou Arianon ethnous despotes eimi"*, which translates to "I am the king of the kingdom (*nation*) of the Iranians". In Middle Persian, Shapur says *"ērānšahr xwadāy hēm"* and in Parthian he says *"aryānšahr xwadāy ahēm"*.[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

The Avesta clearly uses *airiia-* as an ethnic name ([Videvdat](/wiki/Vendidad "Vendidad") 1; [Yasht](/wiki/Yasht "Yasht") 13.143–44, etc.), where it appears in expressions such as *airyāfi daiŋˊhāvō* ("Iranian lands"), *airyō šayanəm* ("land inhabited by Iranians"), and *airyanəm vaējō vaŋhuyāfi dāityayāfi* ("Iranian stretch of the good Dāityā").[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21) In the late part of the [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta") (Videvdat 1), one of the mentioned homelands was referred to as *[Airyan'əm Vaējah](/wiki/Airyanem_Vaejah "Airyanem Vaejah")* which approximately means "expanse of the Iranians". The homeland varied in its geographic range, the area around [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat") ([Pliny](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder")'s view) and even the entire expanse of the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau") ([Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo")'s designation).[[30]](#cite_note-Meyer_1911,_p._742-31)

The Old Persian and Avestan evidence is confirmed by the Greek sources.[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21) [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus"), in his *[Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)")*, remarks about the Iranian Medes that "Medes were called anciently by all people *Arians*" (7.62).[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Bailey-22) In Armenian sources, the Parthians, Medes and Persians are collectively referred to as *Iranians*.[[31]](#cite_note-32) [Eudemus of Rhodes](/wiki/Eudemus_of_Rhodes "Eudemus of Rhodes") (Dubitationes et Solutiones de Primis Principiis, in Platonis Parmenidem) refers to "the *Magi* and all those of *Iranian* (*áreion*) lineage". [Diodorus Siculus](/wiki/Diodorus_Siculus "Diodorus Siculus") (1.94.2) considers Zoroaster (*Zathraustēs*) as one of the *Arianoi*.[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21)

[Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), in his *[Geographica](/wiki/Geographica "Geographica")* (1st century AD), mentions of the [Medes](/wiki/Medes "Medes"), Persians, [Bactrians](/wiki/Bactrians "Bactrians") and [Sogdians](/wiki/Sogdians "Sogdians") of the Iranian Plateau and [Transoxiana](/wiki/Transoxiana "Transoxiana") of antiquity:[[32]](#cite_note-33)

> The name of *[Ariana](/wiki/Ariana "Ariana")* is further extended to a part of [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") and of Media, as also to the [Bactrians](/wiki/Bactrians "Bactrians") and [Sogdians](/wiki/Sogdians "Sogdians") on the north; for these speak approximately the same language, with but slight variations.

— *Geographica*, 15.8

The [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactrian_language "Bactrian language") (a Middle Iranian language) [inscription](/wiki/Rabatak_inscription "Rabatak inscription") of [Kanishka](/wiki/Kanishka "Kanishka") (the founder of the [Kushan Empire](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire")) at Rabatak, which was discovered in 1993 in an unexcavated site in the Afghan province of [Baghlan](/wiki/Baghlan_Province "Baghlan Province"), clearly refers to this [Eastern Iranian language](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages") as *Arya*.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

All this evidence shows that the name *Arya* was a collective definition, denoting peoples who were aware of belonging to the one ethnic stock, speaking a common language, and having a religious tradition that centered on the cult of Ohrmazd.[[21]](#cite_note-iranica.com-21)

The academic usage of the term *Iranian* is distinct from the state of [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") and its various citizens (who are all Iranian by nationality), in the same way that the term *Germanic peoples* is distinct from *[Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans")*. Some inhabitants of Iran are not necessarily ethnic Iranians by virtue of not being speakers of Iranian languages.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Iranian vs. Iranic

Some scholars such as [John Perry](/wiki/John_R._Perry_(orientalist) "John R. Perry (orientalist)") prefer the term *Iranic* as the name for the linguistic family of this category (many of which are spoken outside Iran), while *Iranian* for anything about the country Iran. He uses the same analogue as in differentiating [German](/wiki/German_people "German people") from [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") or differentiating [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") and [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages").[[34]](#cite_note-35) German scholar Martin Kümmel also argues for the same distinction of *Iranian* from *Iranic*.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

## History and settlement

### Indo-European roots

Main articles: [Indo-Iranians](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") and [Proto-Indo-Europeans](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Indo-European_migrations.jpg/330px-Indo-European_migrations.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indo-European_migrations.jpg)

Early [Indo-European migrations](/wiki/Indo-European_migrations "Indo-European migrations") from the [Pontic–Caspian steppe](/wiki/Pontic%E2%80%93Caspian_steppe "Pontic–Caspian steppe") and across Central Asia.

#### Proto-Indo-Iranians

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Indo-Iranian_origins.png/330px-Indo-Iranian_origins.png)](/wiki/File:Indo-Iranian_origins.png)

Archaeological cultures associated with [Indo-Iranian migrations](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_migration "Indo-Iranian migration") (after [EIEC](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Indo-European_Culture "Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture")). The [Andronovo](/wiki/Andronovo_culture "Andronovo culture"), BMAC and [Yaz cultures](/wiki/Yaz_culture "Yaz culture") have often been associated with it. The [GGC](/wiki/Gandhara_grave_culture "Gandhara grave culture") (Swat), [Cemetery H](/wiki/Cemetery_H_culture "Cemetery H culture"), [Copper Hoard](/wiki/Copper_Hoard_Culture "Copper Hoard Culture") and [PGW](/wiki/Painted_Grey_Ware_culture "Painted Grey Ware culture") cultures are candidates for the same associations.

The Proto-Indo-Iranians are commonly identified with the [Sintashta culture](/wiki/Sintashta_culture "Sintashta culture") and the subsequent [Andronovo culture](/wiki/Andronovo_culture "Andronovo culture") within the broader Andronovo horizon, and their homeland with an area of the [Eurasian steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_steppe "Eurasian steppe") that borders the [Ural River](/wiki/Ural_River "Ural River") on the west and the [Tian Shan](/wiki/Tian_Shan "Tian Shan") on the east.

The Indo-Iranian migrations took place in two waves.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow1973-37)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola1999-38) The first wave consisted of the Indo-Aryan migration through the [Bactria-Margiana Culture](/wiki/Bactria%E2%80%93Margiana_Archaeological_Complex "Bactria–Margiana Archaeological Complex"), also called "Bactria-Margiana Archaeological Complex", into the Levant, founding the [Mitanni](/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni") kingdom; and a migration south-eastward of the Vedic people, over the Hindu Kush into northern India.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith2009-39) The Indo-Aryans split off around 1800–1600 BC from the Iranians,[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007408-40) whereafter they were defeated and split into two groups by the Iranians,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith200933_note_20,_p.35-41) who dominated the Central Eurasian steppe zone[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith200933-42) and "chased [the Indo-Aryans] to the extremities of Central Eurasia".[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith200933-42) One group were the Indo-Aryans who founded the [Mitanni](/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni") kingdom in northern Syria;[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid0FDqf415wqgCpgPA454_454]-43) (c. 1500 – c. 1300 BC) the other group were the Vedic people.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith200933_note_20-44) [Christopher I. Beckwith](/wiki/Christopher_I._Beckwith "Christopher I. Beckwith") suggests that the [Wusun](/wiki/Wusun "Wusun"), an [Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans") [Caucasian](/wiki/Caucasian_race "Caucasian race") people of [Inner Asia](/wiki/Inner_Asia "Inner Asia") in [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), were also of Indo-Aryan origin.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith2009376-7-45)

The second wave is interpreted as the Iranian wave,[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMallory198942–43-46) and took place in the third stage of the Indo-European migrations[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith2009-39) from 800 BC onwards.

#### Sintashta–Petrovka culture

Main article: [Sintashta culture](/wiki/Sintashta_culture "Sintashta culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/From_Corded_Ware_to_Sintashta.jpg/330px-From_Corded_Ware_to_Sintashta.jpg)](/wiki/File:From_Corded_Ware_to_Sintashta.jpg)

According to Allentoft (2015), the Sintashta culture probably derived from the Corded Ware culture.

The Sintashta culture, also known as the Sintashta–Petrovka culture[[46]](#cite_note-Koryakova_1998b-47) or Sintashta–Arkaim culture,[[47]](#cite_note-Koryakova_1998a-48) is a [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") [archaeological culture](/wiki/Archaeological_culture "Archaeological culture") of the northern [Eurasian steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_steppe "Eurasian steppe") on the borders of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), dated to the period 2100–1800 [BC](/wiki/Before_Christ "Before Christ").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfEK-BkqXfJACpgPA47_47]-49) It is probably the archaeological manifestation of the Indo-Iranian language group.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid0FDqf415wqgCpgPA390_390]_(fig._15.9),_[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid0FDqf415wqgCpgPA406_405–411]-50)

The Sintashta culture emerged from the interaction of two antecedent cultures. Its immediate predecessor in the Ural-Tobol steppe was the [Poltavka culture](/wiki/Poltavka_culture "Poltavka culture"), an offshoot of the cattle-herding [Yamnaya horizon](/wiki/Yamna_culture "Yamna culture") that moved east into the region between 2800 and 2600 BC. Several Sintashta towns were built over older Poltavka settlements or close to Poltavka cemeteries, and Poltavka motifs are common on Sintashta pottery. Sintashta [material culture](/wiki/Material_culture "Material culture") also shows the influence of the late [Abashevo culture](/wiki/Abashevo_culture "Abashevo culture"), a collection of [Corded Ware](/wiki/Corded_Ware "Corded Ware") settlements in the [forest steppe](/wiki/Forest_steppe "Forest steppe") zone north of the Sintashta region that were also predominantly [pastoralist](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid0FDqf415wqgCpgPA385_385–388]-51) Allentoft et al. (2015) also found close [autosomal](/wiki/AuDNA "AuDNA") genetic relationship between peoples of [Corded Ware culture](/wiki/Corded_Ware_culture "Corded Ware culture") and Sintashta culture.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

The earliest known [chariots](/wiki/Chariot "Chariot") have been found in Sintashta burials, and the culture is considered a strong candidate for the origin of the technology, which spread throughout the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") and played an important role in [ancient warfare](/wiki/Ancient_warfare "Ancient warfare").[[52]](#cite_note-Kuznetsov_2006-53) Sintashta settlements are also remarkable for the intensity of [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") mining and [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy") carried out there, which is unusual for a steppe culture.[[53]](#cite_note-Hanks_&_Linduff_2009-54)

Because of the difficulty of identifying the remains of Sintashta sites beneath those of later settlements, the culture was only recently distinguished from the [Andronovo culture](/wiki/Andronovo_culture "Andronovo culture").[[47]](#cite_note-Koryakova_1998a-48) It is now recognised as a separate entity forming part of the 'Andronovo horizon'.[[46]](#cite_note-Koryakova_1998b-47)

#### Andronovo culture

Main article: [Andronovo culture](/wiki/Andronovo_culture "Andronovo culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Andronovo_culture.png/330px-Andronovo_culture.png)](/wiki/File:Andronovo_culture.png)

The Andronovo culture's approximate maximal extent, with the formative Sintashta-Petrovka culture (red), the location of the earliest [spoke](/wiki/Spoke "Spoke")-wheeled [chariot](/wiki/Chariot "Chariot") finds (purple), and the adjacent and overlapping [Afanasevo](/wiki/Afanasevo_culture "Afanasevo culture"), [Srubna](/wiki/Srubna_culture "Srubna culture"), and [BMAC](/wiki/Bactria%E2%80%93Margiana_Archaeological_Complex "Bactria–Margiana Archaeological Complex") cultures (green).

The Andronovo culture is a collection of similar local [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") [Indo-Iranian](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") cultures that flourished c. 1800–900 BC in western [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and the west [Asiatic steppe](/wiki/Asian_Steppe "Asian Steppe").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMallory199720–21-55) It is probably better termed an archaeological complex or [archaeological horizon](/wiki/Archaeological_horizon "Archaeological horizon"). The name derives from the village of Andronovo ([55°53′N 55°42′E﻿ / ﻿55.883°N 55.700°E﻿ / 55.883; 55.700](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Iranian_peoples&params=55_53_N_55_42_E_)), where in 1914, several graves were discovered, with skeletons in crouched positions, buried with richly decorated pottery. The older [Sintashta culture](/wiki/Sintashta_culture "Sintashta culture") (2100–1800), formerly included within the Andronovo culture, is now considered separately, but regarded as its predecessor, and accepted as part of the wider Andronovo horizon. At least four sub-cultures of the Andronovo horizon have been distinguished, during which the culture expands towards the south and the east:

* **Sintashta-Petrovka-Arkaim** (Southern [Urals](/wiki/Urals "Urals"), northern [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), 2200–1600 BC)
  + the [Sintashta](/wiki/Sintashta "Sintashta") fortification of ca. 1800 BC in [Chelyabinsk Oblast](/wiki/Chelyabinsk_Oblast "Chelyabinsk Oblast")
  + the [Petrovka settlement](/wiki/Petrovka_settlement "Petrovka settlement") fortified settlement in Kazakhstan
  + the nearby [Arkaim](/wiki/Arkaim "Arkaim") settlement dated to the 17th century
* **Alakul** (2100–1400 BC) between [Oxus](/wiki/Oxus "Oxus") and [Jaxartes](/wiki/Jaxartes "Jaxartes"), [Kyzylkum desert](/wiki/Kyzylkum_desert "Kyzylkum desert")
  + **Alekseyevka** (1300–1100 BC "final Bronze") in eastern Kazakhstan, contacts with [Namazga](/wiki/Namazga "Namazga") VI in Turkmenia
  + [Ingala Valley](/wiki/Ingala_Valley "Ingala Valley") in the south of the [Tyumen Oblast](/wiki/Tyumen_Oblast "Tyumen Oblast")
* **Fedorovo** (1500–1300 BC) in southern Siberia (earliest evidence of [cremation](/wiki/Cremation "Cremation") and [fire cult](/wiki/Fire_worship "Fire worship"))[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDiakonoffKuz'minaIvantchik1995473-56)
  + [Beshkent](/wiki/Beshkent_district "Beshkent district")-[Vakhsh](/wiki/Vakhsh,_Tajikistan "Vakhsh, Tajikistan") (1000–800 BC)

The geographical extent of the culture is vast and difficult to delineate exactly. On its western fringes, it overlaps with the approximately contemporaneous, but distinct, [Srubna culture](/wiki/Srubna_culture "Srubna culture") in the [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga")-[Ural](/wiki/Ural_River "Ural River") interfluvial. To the east, it reaches into the [Minusinsk](/wiki/Minusinsk "Minusinsk") depression, with some sites as far west as the southern [Ural Mountains](/wiki/Ural_Mountains "Ural Mountains"),[[56]](#cite_note-camhist-57) overlapping with the area of the earlier [Afanasevo culture](/wiki/Afanasevo_culture "Afanasevo culture").[[57]](#cite_note-58) Additional sites are scattered as far south as the [Koppet Dag](/wiki/Koppet_Dag "Koppet Dag") ([Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan")), the [Pamir](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains") ([Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan")) and the [Tian Shan](/wiki/Tian_Shan "Tian Shan") ([Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan")). The northern boundary vaguely corresponds to the beginning of the [Taiga](/wiki/Taiga "Taiga").[[56]](#cite_note-camhist-57) In the Volga basin, interaction with the Srubna culture was the most intense and prolonged, and Federovo style pottery is found as far west as [Volgograd](/wiki/Volgograd "Volgograd").

Most researchers associate the Andronovo horizon with early [Indo-Iranian languages](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages"), though it may have overlapped the early [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages")-speaking area at its northern fringe.

The archeological features of the [Yaz I culture](/wiki/Yaz_culture "Yaz culture") are seen as the results of the intrusion of nomadic Indo-Iranians from the northern Andronovo culture and their interaction with indigenous traditions from the preceding [BMAC culture](/wiki/Bactria%E2%80%93Margiana_Archaeological_Complex "Bactria–Margiana Archaeological Complex").[[58]](#cite_note-Yaz_culture-59)

### Scythians and Persians

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/PazyrikHorseman.JPG/250px-PazyrikHorseman.JPG)](/wiki/File:PazyrikHorseman.JPG)

[Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka") horseman, [Pazyryk](/wiki/Pazyryk_culture "Pazyryk culture"), from a carpet, c. 300 BC

From the late 2nd millennium BC to early 1st millennium BC the Iranians had expanded from the [Eurasian Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe "Eurasian Steppe"), and Iranian peoples such as [Medes](/wiki/Medes "Medes"), [Persians](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people"), [Parthians](/wiki/Parthia "Parthia") and [Bactrians](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria") populated the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau").[[59]](#cite_note-60)

Scythian tribes, along with [Cimmerians](/wiki/Cimmerians "Cimmerians"), [Sarmatians](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians") and [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans") populated the [steppes](/wiki/Pontic%E2%80%93Caspian_steppe "Pontic–Caspian steppe") north of the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"). The [Scythian](/wiki/Scythia "Scythia") and Sarmatian tribes were spread across [Great Hungarian Plain](/wiki/Great_Hungarian_Plain "Great Hungarian Plain"), South-Eastern Ukraine, Russias [Siberian](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Russia "Southern Russia"), [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga"),[[60]](#cite_note-61) [Uralic](/wiki/Ural_(region) "Ural (region)") regions and the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"),[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) while other Scythian tribes, such as the [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka"), spread as far east as [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"), China.

### Western and Eastern Iranians

The division into an "[Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian "Eastern Iranian")" and a "[Western](/wiki/Western_Iranian "Western Iranian")" group by the early 1st millennium is visible in [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan_language "Avestan language") vs. [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian_language "Old Persian language"), the two oldest known Iranian languages.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Old Avestan texts known as the [Gathas](/wiki/Gathas "Gathas") are believed to have been composed by [Zoroaster](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster"), the founder of [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), with the [Yaz culture](/wiki/Yaz_culture "Yaz culture") (c. 1500 BC – 1100 BC) as a candidate for the development of Eastern Iranian culture.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuz'mina2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidjuivCQAAQBAJpgPA449_449]-65)

#### Western Iranian peoples

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Assimilation_of_Baltic_and_Aryan_Peoples_by_Uralic_Speakers_in_the_Middle_and_Upper_Volga_Basin_%28Shaded_Relief_BG%29.png/250px-Assimilation_of_Baltic_and_Aryan_Peoples_by_Uralic_Speakers_in_the_Middle_and_Upper_Volga_Basin_%28Shaded_Relief_BG%29.png)](/wiki/File:Assimilation_of_Baltic_and_Aryan_Peoples_by_Uralic_Speakers_in_the_Middle_and_Upper_Volga_Basin_(Shaded_Relief_BG).png)

Distribution of Iranic peoples during the Iron Age

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Map_of_the_Achaemenid_Empire.jpg/330px-Map_of_the_Achaemenid_Empire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Achaemenid_Empire.jpg)

[Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") at its greatest extent under the rule of [Darius I](/wiki/Darius_I "Darius I") (522–486 BC)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Persepolis-Darafsh_1_%2816%29.JPG/250px-Persepolis-Darafsh_1_%2816%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Persepolis-Darafsh_1_(16).JPG)

[Persepolis](/wiki/Persepolis "Persepolis"): Persian guards

During the 1st centuries of the 1st millennium BC, the ancient Persians established themselves in the western portion of the Iranian Plateau and appear to have interacted considerably with the Elamites and [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylonians "Babylonians"), while the [Medes](/wiki/Medes "Medes") also entered in contact with the [Assyrians](/wiki/Ancient_Assyria "Ancient Assyria").[[65]](#cite_note-66) Remnants of the [Median language](/wiki/Median_language "Median language") and [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian") show their common Proto-Iranian roots, emphasized in Strabo and Herodotus' description of their languages as very similar to the languages spoken by the Bactrians and [Sogdians](/wiki/Sogdiana "Sogdiana") in the east.[[30]](#cite_note-Meyer_1911,_p._742-31)[[66]](#cite_note-Geo_Strabo-67) Following the establishment of the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_dynasty "Achaemenid dynasty"), the Persian language (referred to as "*Farsi*" in Persian after being changed from *Parsi*) spread from Pars or [Fars province](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province") (Persia) to various regions of the Empire, with the modern dialects of Iran, Afghanistan (also known as [Dari](/wiki/Dari_(Afghanistan) "Dari (Afghanistan)")) and Central-Asia (known as [Tajiki](/wiki/Tajik_language "Tajik language")) descending from Old Persian.

At first, the Western Iranian peoples in the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") were dominated by the various [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") empires. An alliance of the Medes with the [Persians](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people"), and rebelling [Babylonians](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian_empire "Neo-Babylonian empire"), [Scythians](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians"), [Chaldeans](/wiki/Chaldea "Chaldea"), and [Cimmerians](/wiki/Cimmerians "Cimmerians"), helped the Medes to capture [Nineveh](/wiki/Nineveh "Nineveh") in 612 BC, which resulted in the eventual collapse of the [Neo-Assyrian Empire](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire") by 605 BC.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The Medes were subsequently able to establish their Median kingdom (with [Ecbatana](/wiki/Ecbatana "Ecbatana") as their royal centre) beyond their original homeland and had eventually a territory stretching roughly from northeastern Iran to the [Halys River](/wiki/Halys_River "Halys River") in [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"). After the fall of the Assyrian Empire, between 616 BC and 605 BC, a unified Median state was formed, which, together with [Babylonia](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia"), [Lydia](/wiki/Lydia "Lydia"), and [Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), became one of the four major powers of the [ancient Near East](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East")

Later on, in 550 BC, [Cyrus the Great](/wiki/Cyrus_the_Great "Cyrus the Great"), would overthrow the leading Median rule, and conquer [Kingdom of Lydia](/wiki/Lydia "Lydia") and the Babylonian Empire after which he established the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") (or the First Persian Empire), while his successors would dramatically extend its borders. At its greatest extent, the Achaemenid Empire would encompass swaths of territory across three continents, namely Europe, Africa and Asia, stretching from the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") proper in the west, to the [Indus Valley](/wiki/Indus_Valley "Indus Valley") in the east. The largest empire of [ancient history](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), with their base in [Persis](/wiki/Persis "Persis") (although the main capital was located in Babylon) the Achaemenids would rule much of the known ancient world for centuries. This First Persian Empire was equally notable for its successful model of a centralised, bureaucratic administration (through [satraps](/wiki/Satrap "Satrap") under a [king](/wiki/King_of_Kings "King of Kings")) and a government working to the profit of its subjects, for building infrastructure such as a [postal system](/wiki/Chapar_Khaneh "Chapar Khaneh") and [road systems](/wiki/Royal_road "Royal road") and the use of an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") across its territories and a large professional army and civil services (inspiring similar systems in later empires),[[68]](#cite_note-schmitt-EI-i-69) and for emancipation of slaves including the [Jewish exiles in Babylon](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"), and is noted in Western history as the antagonist of the [Greek city states](/wiki/Polis "Polis") during the [Greco-Persian Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars"). The [Mausoleum at Halicarnassus](/wiki/Mausoleum_at_Halicarnassus "Mausoleum at Halicarnassus"), one of the [Seven Wonders of the Ancient World](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_the_Ancient_World "Seven Wonders of the Ancient World"), was built in the empire as well.

The Greco-Persian Wars resulted in the Persians being forced to withdraw from their [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") territories, setting the direct further course of history of [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") and the rest of Europe. More than a century later, a prince of [Macedon](/wiki/Macedonia_(ancient_kingdom) "Macedonia (ancient kingdom)") (which itself was a subject to Persia from the late 6th century BC up to the [First Persian invasion of Greece](/wiki/First_Persian_invasion_of_Greece "First Persian invasion of Greece")) later known by the name of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great"), overthrew the incumbent Persian king, by which the Achaemenid Empire was ended.

Old Persian is attested in the [Behistun Inscription](/wiki/Behistun_Inscription "Behistun Inscription") (c. 519 BC), recording a proclamation by [Darius the Great](/wiki/Darius_the_Great "Darius the Great").[[69]](#cite_note-Lubotsky-70) In southwestern Iran, the [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid "Achaemenid") kings usually wrote their inscriptions in trilingual form ([Elamite](/wiki/Elamite_language "Elamite language"), [Babylonian](/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") and [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian_language "Old Persian language"))[[70]](#cite_note-71) while elsewhere other languages were used. The administrative languages were Elamite in the early period, and later [Imperial Aramaic](/wiki/Imperial_Aramaic "Imperial Aramaic"),[[71]](#cite_note-72) as well as [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"), making it a widely used [bureaucratic](/wiki/Bureaucratic "Bureaucratic") language.[[72]](#cite_note-Iranian,_E._Tucker_2001-73) Even though the Achaemenids had extensive contacts with the Greeks and vice versa, and had conquered many of the Greek-speaking area's both in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor") during different periods of the empire, the native Old Iranian sources provide no indication of Greek linguistic evidence.[[72]](#cite_note-Iranian,_E._Tucker_2001-73) However, there is plenty of evidence (in addition to the accounts of Herodotus) that Greeks, apart from being deployed and employed in the core regions of the empire, also evidently lived and worked in the heartland of the Achaemenid Empire, namely Iran.[[72]](#cite_note-Iranian,_E._Tucker_2001-73) For example, Greeks were part of the various ethnicities that constructed Darius' palace in [Susa](/wiki/Susa "Susa"), apart from the Greek inscriptions found nearby there, and one short Persepolis tablet written in Greek.[[72]](#cite_note-Iranian,_E._Tucker_2001-73)

The early inhabitants of the Achaemenid Empire appear to have adopted the religion of [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism").[[73]](#cite_note-Edinburgh-74) The [Baloch](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people") who speak a west Iranian language relate an oral tradition regarding their migration from [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") around the year 1000 AD, whereas linguistic evidence links [Balochi](/wiki/Balochi_language "Balochi language") to [Kurmanji](/wiki/Kurmanji "Kurmanji"), [Soranî](/wiki/Soran%C3%AE "Soranî"), [Gorani](/wiki/Gorani_language_(Zaza-Gorani) "Gorani language (Zaza-Gorani)") and [Zaza language](/wiki/Zaza_language "Zaza language").[[74]](#cite_note-Rezakhani-75)

#### Eastern Iranian peoples

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Balto-Slavic_lng.png/330px-Balto-Slavic_lng.png)](/wiki/File:Balto-Slavic_lng.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Eastern_and_Central_Europe_around_750_BC.png/330px-Eastern_and_Central_Europe_around_750_BC.png)](/wiki/File:Eastern_and_Central_Europe_around_750_BC.png)

Archaeological cultures c. 750 BC at the start of Eastern-Central Europe's [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"); the Proto-Scythian culture borders the Balto-Slavic cultures ([Lusatian](/wiki/Lusatian_culture "Lusatian culture"), [Milograd](/wiki/Milograd_culture "Milograd culture") and [Chernoles](/wiki/Chernoles_culture "Chernoles culture"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/AzesIITriratna.jpg/250px-AzesIITriratna.jpg)](/wiki/File:AzesIITriratna.jpg)

Silver coin of the [Indo-Scythian](/wiki/Indo-Scythian "Indo-Scythian") king [Azes II](/wiki/Azes_II "Azes II") (reigned c. 35–12 BC). Buddhist [triratna](/wiki/Triratna "Triratna") symbol in the left field on the reverse

While the Iranian tribes of the south are better known through their texts and modern counterparts, the tribes which remained largely in the vast Eurasian expanse are known through the references made to them by the ancient Greeks, Persians, Chinese, and Indo-Aryans as well as by archaeological finds. The [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") chronicler, [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") (5th century BC) makes references to a nomadic people, the [Scythians](/wiki/Scythia "Scythia"); he describes them as having dwelt in what is today southern European Russia and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). He was the first to make a reference to them.
Many ancient [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") texts from a later period make references to such tribes they were witness of pointing them towards the southeasternmost edges of Central Asia, around the [Hindukush](/wiki/Hindukush "Hindukush") range in northern Pakistan.

It is believed that these Scythians were conquered by their eastern cousins, the [Sarmatians](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians"), who are mentioned by [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") as the dominant tribe which controlled the southern Russian steppe in the 1st millennium AD. These Sarmatians were also known to the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), who conquered the western tribes in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") and sent Sarmatian conscripts, as part of Roman legions, as far west as [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"). These Iranian-speaking Scythians and Sarmatians dominated large parts of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") for a millennium, and were eventually absorbed and assimilated (e.g. [Slavicisation](/wiki/Slavicisation "Slavicisation")) by the [Proto](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs")-[Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") population of the region.[[8]](#cite_note-The_Sarmatians,_600_BC-AD_450-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Taylor_&_Francis-9)[[11]](#cite_note-Slovene_Studies-11)

The Sarmatians differed from the Scythians in their veneration of the god of fire rather than god of nature, and [women's prominent role](/wiki/Women_in_ancient_warfare "Women in ancient warfare") in warfare, which possibly served as the inspiration for the [Amazons](/wiki/Amazons "Amazons").[[75]](#cite_note-EBSarmatian-76)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuliaev2003-77) At their greatest reported extent, around the 1st century AD, these tribes ranged from the [Vistula River](/wiki/Vistula_River "Vistula River") to the mouth of the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") and eastward to the [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga"), bordering the shores of the [Black](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") and [Caspian](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea") Seas as well as the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") to the south.[[b]](#cite_note-78) Their territory, which was known as Sarmatia to [Greco-Roman ethnographers](/w/index.php?title=Greco-Roman_ethnographers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Greco-Roman ethnographers (page does not exist)"), corresponded to the western part of greater Scythia (mostly modern [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") and [Southern Russia](/wiki/Southern_Federal_District "Southern Federal District"), also to a smaller extent north eastern Balkans around [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova")). According to authors Arrowsmith, Fellowes and Graves Hansard in their book *A Grammar of Ancient Geography* published in 1832, Sarmatia had two parts, [Sarmatia Europea](/wiki/Sarmatia_Europea "Sarmatia Europea")[[77]](#cite_note-79) and [Sarmatia Asiatica](/wiki/Sarmatia_Asiatica "Sarmatia Asiatica")[[78]](#cite_note-80) covering a combined area of 503,000 sq mi or 1,302,764 km2.

Throughout the 1st millennium AD, the large presence of the Sarmatians who once dominated Ukraine, [Southern Russia](/wiki/Southern_Russia "Southern Russia"), and swaths of the [Carpathians](/wiki/Carpathians "Carpathians"), gradually started to diminish mainly due to assimilation and absorption by the [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths"), especially from the areas near the Roman frontier, but only completely by the Proto-Slavic peoples. The abundant East Iranian-derived [toponyms](/wiki/Toponyms "Toponyms") in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") proper (e.g. some of the largest rivers; the [Dniestr](/wiki/Dniestr "Dniestr") and [Dniepr](/wiki/Dniepr "Dniepr")), as well as loanwords adopted predominantly through the [Eastern Slavic languages](/wiki/Eastern_Slavic_languages "Eastern Slavic languages") and adopted aspects of Iranian culture amongst the early Slavs, are all a remnant of this. A connection between [Proto-Slavonic](/wiki/Proto-Slavonic "Proto-Slavonic") and Iranian languages is also furthermore proven by the earliest layer of [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") in the former.[[79]](#cite_note-harvtxt|Sussex|2011|p=109-81) For instance, the Proto-Slavonic words for god *(\*bogъ)*, demon *(\*divъ)*, house *(\*xata)*, axe *(\*toporъ)* and dog *(\*sobaka)* are of [Scythian](/wiki/Scythian_languages "Scythian languages") origin.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

The extensive contact between these Scytho-Sarmatian Iranian tribes in Eastern Europe and the (Early) Slavs included religion. After Slavic and Baltic languages diverged the Early Slavs interacted with Iranian peoples and merged elements of Iranian spirituality into their beliefs. For example, both Early Iranian and Slavic supreme gods were considered givers of wealth, unlike the supreme thunder gods in many other European religions. Also, both Slavs and Iranians had demons –- given names from similar linguistic roots, Daêva (Iranian) and Divŭ (Slavic) –- and a concept of [dualism](/wiki/Dualistic_cosmology "Dualistic cosmology"), of good and evil.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

The Sarmatians of the east, based in the [Pontic–Caspian steppe](/wiki/Pontic%E2%80%93Caspian_steppe "Pontic–Caspian steppe"), became the [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans"), who also ventured far and wide, with a branch ending up in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and then [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), as they accompanied the Germanic [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals") and [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") during their migrations. The modern [Ossetians](/wiki/Ossetians "Ossetians") are believed to be the direct descendants of the Alans, as other remnants of the Alans disappeared following Germanic, [Hunnic](/wiki/Huns "Huns") and ultimately Slavic migrations and invasions.[[82]](#cite_note-ISBN-84)
Another group of Alans allied with Goths to defeat the Romans and ultimately settled in what is now called Catalonia (Goth-Alania).[[83]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/bd/HormizdICoinHistoryofIran.jpg)](/wiki/File:HormizdICoinHistoryofIran.jpg)

[Hormizd I](/wiki/Hormizd_I "Hormizd I"), Sassanian coin

Some of the Saka-Scythian tribes in Central Asia would later move further southeast and invade the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau"), large sections of present-day [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and finally deep into present day Pakistan (see [Indo-Scythians](/wiki/Indo-Scythians "Indo-Scythians")). Another Iranian tribe related to the Saka-Scythians were the [Parni](/wiki/Parni "Parni") in Central Asia, and who later become indistinguishable from the [Parthians](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire"), speakers of a northwest-Iranian language. Many Iranian tribes, including the [Khwarazmians](/wiki/Khwarazm "Khwarazm"), [Massagetae](/wiki/Massagetae "Massagetae") and [Sogdians](/wiki/Sogdiana "Sogdiana"), were assimilated and/or displaced in Central Asia by the migrations of [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_people "Turkic people") tribes emanating out of Xinjiang and Siberia.[[84]](#cite_note-Kimball-86)

The modern [Sarikoli](/wiki/Sarikoli_language "Sarikoli language") in southern Xinjiang and the Ossetians of the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") (mainly [South Ossetia](/wiki/South_Ossetia "South Ossetia") and [North Ossetia](/wiki/North_Ossetia "North Ossetia")) are remnants of the various Scythian-derived tribes from the vast far and wide territory they once dwelled in. The modern [Ossetians](/wiki/Ossetians "Ossetians") are the descendants of the Alano-Sarmatians,[[85]](#cite_note-OEMN-87)[[86]](#cite_note-encarta20082-88) and their claims are supported by their Northeast Iranian language, while culturally the Ossetians resemble their [North Caucasian](/wiki/North_Caucasus "North Caucasus") neighbors, the [Kabardians](/wiki/Kabard "Kabard") and [Circassians](/wiki/Circassians "Circassians").[[82]](#cite_note-ISBN-84)[[87]](#cite_note-89) Various extinct Iranian peoples existed in the eastern Caucasus, including the [Azaris](/wiki/Ancient_Azari_language "Ancient Azari language"), while some Iranian peoples remain in the region, including the [Talysh](/wiki/Talysh_people "Talysh people")[[88]](#cite_note-Talysh-90) and the [Tats](/wiki/Tat_people_(Caucasus) "Tat people (Caucasus)")[[89]](#cite_note-Tats-91) found in Azerbaijan and as far north as the Russian republic of [Dagestan](/wiki/Dagestan "Dagestan"). A remnant of the Sogdians is found in the Yaghnobi-speaking population in parts of the Zeravshan valley in Tajikistan.

### Later developments

The main [migration](/wiki/Turkic_migration "Turkic migration") of [Turkic peoples](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") occurred between the 6th and 10th centuries, when they spread across most of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"). The Turkic peoples slowly replaced and assimilated the previous Iranian-speaking locals, turning the population of Central Asia from being largely [Iranian](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") into being primarily of East Asian descent.[[90]](#cite_note-92)

Starting with the reign of [Omar](/wiki/Umar "Umar") in 634 AD, [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") [Arabs](/wiki/Arab "Arab") began a conquest of the Iranian Plateau. The Arabs conquered the [Sassanid Empire](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire") of the Persians and seized much of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") populated by the [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds") and others. Ultimately, the various Iranian peoples, including the Persians, Pashtuns, Kurds and Balochis, converted to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), while the [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans") converted to [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), thus laying the foundation for the fact that the modern-day Ossetians are [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoltz2022-93)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The Iranian peoples would later split along sectarian lines as the Persians adopted the [Shi'a](/wiki/SHIA_Islam "SHIA Islam") sect. As ancient tribes and identities changed, so did the Iranian peoples, many of whom assimilated foreign cultures and peoples.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

Later, during the 2nd millennium AD, the Iranian peoples would play a prominent role during the age of Islamic expansion and empire. [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin"), a noted adversary of the [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusade "Crusade"), was an ethnic [Kurd](/wiki/Kurd "Kurd"), while various empires centered in Iran (including the [Safavids](/wiki/Safavid_dynasty "Safavid dynasty")) re-established a modern dialect of Persian as the official language spoken throughout much of what is today Iran and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"). Iranian influence was also an principal factor in the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). The Ottoman Turks integrated Persian into their court, governance, and daily life. Supported by the sultans, nobility, and spiritual leaders, Persian was promoted as a second language, intertwining with Turkish and greatly influencing Ottoman cultural traditions.[[93]](#cite_note-95) However, a heavy [Turko-Persian](/wiki/Turko-Persian_tradition "Turko-Persian tradition") basis in Anatolia was set already by the predecessors of the Ottomans, namely the [Sultanate of Rum](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Rum "Sultanate of Rum") and [Anatolian Beyliks](/wiki/Anatolian_Beyliks "Anatolian Beyliks") amongst others) as well to the court of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"). All of the major Iranian peoples reasserted their use of Iranian languages following the decline of Arab rule, but would not begin to form modern [national](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") identities until the 19th and early 20th centuries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Persian nationalism

Main article: [Persian nationalism](/wiki/Persian_nationalism "Persian nationalism")

See also: [Iranian nationalism](/wiki/Iranian_nationalism "Iranian nationalism"), [Pan-Iranism](/wiki/Pan-Iranism "Pan-Iranism"), and [Aryanism](/wiki/Aryanism "Aryanism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Distribution_of_Iranian_Languages.png/330px-Distribution_of_Iranian_Languages.png)](/wiki/File:Distribution_of_Iranian_Languages.png)

Geographic distribution of modern [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages")

The term "[Persian](/wiki/Persians "Persians")" ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): فُرس, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Furs*, [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): فارس, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Persian "Romanization of Persian"): *Fars*) is more often used in English partly due to the fact that "Iran" was known in the western world as "Persia". In 1959, the government of [Mohammad Reza Pahlavi](/wiki/Mohammad_Reza_Pahlavi "Mohammad Reza Pahlavi"), Reza Shah's son, announced that both "Persia" and "Iran" could officially be used interchangeably.[[94]](#cite_note-yarshater1-96) Nowadays, the term "[Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians")" mainly refers to those whose [mother tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language") is [Persian (Farsi)](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and those who identify as Persian.[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) However, Iran is a [mosaic of ethnic and linguistic groups](/wiki/Demographics_of_Iran "Demographics of Iran").[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) Persians are said to make up roughly half the population (with some estimates reaching 60%), while the rest comprises [Azeris](/wiki/Iranian_Azerbaijanis "Iranian Azerbaijanis"), Arabs (e.g. [Khuzestani Arabs](/wiki/Khuzestani_Arabs "Khuzestani Arabs")), [Balochis](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people"), [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds"), [Gilanis](/wiki/Gilaks "Gilaks"), [Mazanderanis](/wiki/Mazanderani_people "Mazanderani people"), [Loris](/wiki/Lurs "Lurs"), [Qashqais](/wiki/Qashqai_people "Qashqai people"), [Bakhtiaris](/wiki/Bakhtiari_people "Bakhtiari people"), [Armenians](/wiki/Iranian_Armenians "Iranian Armenians"), and [others](/wiki/Ethnicities_in_Iran "Ethnicities in Iran").[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) Although many of these groups speak [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") (Farsi) and identify as Iranian, their ethnic identity is distinct from being Persian. Additionally, Iran is home to various religious minorities—[Sunni Muslims](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"), [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), [Bahá’ís](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), [Zoroastrians](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), and others—some of whom identify as [Persian](/wiki/Persians "Persians") while others do not.[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) The denial of this diversity stems not only from ignorance but also from [Persian-centric nationalism](/wiki/Persian_nationalism "Persian nationalism") rooted in mid-20th century Iranian state policies. This approach, particularly under the [Pahlavi regime](/wiki/Pahlavi_dynasty "Pahlavi dynasty"), sought to erase ethnic and linguistic [diversity](/wiki/Diversity,_equity,_and_inclusion "Diversity, equity, and inclusion") in favour of an [exclusivist Persian identity](/wiki/Persians "Persians").[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97)

Inspired by European and Turkish [nationalist ideologies](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism"), [Reza Shah](/wiki/Reza_Shah "Reza Shah") Pahlavi's regime crafted an artificial narrative of Iranian history centered on Persian ethnic unity over 2,500 years.[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) This contradicted the historical reality, as previous Iranian dynasties, such as the [Qajars](/wiki/Qajar_dynasty "Qajar dynasty") and [Safavids](/wiki/Safavid_dynasty "Safavid dynasty"), were of [Azeri Turkish](/wiki/Azerbaijanis "Azerbaijanis") origin, and the Persian Empire itself historically united diverse peoples through imperial administration and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") rather than [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity").[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) This nationalistic approach extended as far as to the [Gulf Arab states](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf") where the Iranian migrants lived; as such, anything that happened in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") that was annoying to [these countries](/wiki/Gulf_Cooperation_Council "Gulf Cooperation Council"), the pressure was immediately put on [Iranians living in Bahrain](/wiki/Ajam_of_Bahrain "Ajam of Bahrain"), [in Kuwait](/wiki/%27Ajam_of_Kuwait "'Ajam of Kuwait"), or the [rest of the Gulf](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf") in general.[[96]](#cite_note-:9-98): 44–45 Reza Shah's policies were mainly influenced by [Aryanism](/wiki/Aryanism "Aryanism"), a [colonial](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism")-era [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology") linking [language](/wiki/Language "Language") with [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity").[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) This framework, which tied the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") to an imagined migration of an Aryan nation, shaped nationalist projects in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran").[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) [Aryanism](/wiki/Aryanism "Aryanism") conveniently justified European colonial views of Indian and Persian civilizations while influencing [Iranian nationalism](/wiki/Iranian_nationalism "Iranian nationalism") to adopt an exclusionary identity framework.[[95]](#cite_note-:33-97) Author Mehran Kokherdi [Author of "History of South Fars"] states that the term [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians") is used to refer to all groups with original Parsi roots, including the inhabitants of villages scattered across Persia who still speak their [ancient Parsi language](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian"). However, the term *has also come* to describe the populations of major cities (e.g. [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran"), [Shiraz](/wiki/Shiraz "Shiraz"), [Isfahan](/wiki/Isfahan "Isfahan")) more broadly, who consist of a blend of [various ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnicities_in_Iran "Ethnicities in Iran"), all unified by their use of [Modern Persian](/wiki/New_Persian "New Persian")—a [language](/wiki/Iranian_Persian "Iranian Persian") that incorporates elements from [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [Mongolian](/wiki/Mongolian_language "Mongolian language"), and Parsi. Based on their shared language, the people of Iran generally identify them as Persians.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-99): 3–4 This leads many scholars to believe that the term "Iranian" is more encompassing and inclusive of these various ethnic groups (Iranic people, and [ethnic groups in Iran](/wiki/Ethnicities_in_Iran "Ethnicities in Iran")).[[98]](#cite_note-BozorgMehr2-100) It's worth noting that many groups such as the [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds"), do not refer to themselves as such (Persian), despite their Iranic/Iranian roots.

## Demographics

See also: [List of ancient Iranian peoples](/wiki/List_of_ancient_Iranian_peoples "List of ancient Iranian peoples") and [Iranian diaspora](/wiki/Iranian_diaspora "Iranian diaspora")

There are an estimated 150 to 200 million native speakers of Iranian languages, the six major groups of [Persians](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people"), [Lurs](/wiki/Lurs "Lurs"), [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds"), [Tajiks](/wiki/Tajiks "Tajiks"), [Baloch](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people"), and [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") accounting for about 90% of this number.[[99]](#cite_note-101) Currently, most of these Iranian peoples live in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") (mainly [Ossetia](/wiki/Ossetia "Ossetia"), other parts of [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"), [Dagestan](/wiki/Dagestan "Dagestan"), and [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan")), [Iraqi Kurdistan](/wiki/Iraqi_Kurdistan "Iraqi Kurdistan") and [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_people "Kurdish people") majority populated areas of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"). There are also Iranian peoples living in [Eastern Arabia](/wiki/Eastern_Arabia "Eastern Arabia") such as [northern Oman](/wiki/Musandam_Peninsula "Musandam Peninsula"), [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"), and [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait").

Due to recent migrations, there are also large communities of speakers of [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages") in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").
