## Culture

See also: [Proto-Indo-European society](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_society "Proto-Indo-European society")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B7%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%80%D1%83%D0%B7%2C_%D0%A2%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B6%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD_1.jpg/250px-%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B7%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%80%D1%83%D0%B7%2C_%D0%A2%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B6%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B7%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%80%D1%83%D0%B7,_%D0%A2%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B6%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD_1.jpg)

[Nowruz](/wiki/Nowruz "Nowruz"), an ancient Iranian annual festival that is still widely celebrated throughout the Iranian Plateau and beyond, in [Dushanbe](/wiki/Dushanbe "Dushanbe"), [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan").

Iranian culture is today considered to be centered in what is called the *[Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau")*, and has its origins tracing back to the [Andronovo culture](/wiki/Andronovo_culture "Andronovo culture") of the late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), which is associated with other cultures of the [Eurasian Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe "Eurasian Steppe").[[131]](#cite_note-LANDS_OF_IRAN-135)[[132]](#cite_note-136) It was, however, later developed distinguishably from its earlier generations in the Steppe, where a large number of Iranian-speaking peoples (i.e., the [Scythians](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians")) continued to participate, resulting in a differentiation that is displayed in [Iranian mythology](/wiki/Persian_mythology "Persian mythology") as the contrast between [Iran and Turan](/wiki/Turan "Turan").[[131]](#cite_note-LANDS_OF_IRAN-135)

Like other Indo-Europeans, the early Iranians practiced ritual sacrifice, had a social hierarchy consisting of warriors, clerics, and farmers, and recounted their deeds through poetic hymns and sagas.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMallory1989[httpsarchiveorgdetailsinsearchofindoeu00jpmapage112_112–127]-137) Various common traits can be discerned among the Iranian peoples. For instance, the social event of [Nowruz](/wiki/Nowruz "Nowruz") is an ancient Iranian festival that is still celebrated by nearly all of the Iranian peoples. However, due to their different environmental adaptations through migration, the Iranian peoples embrace some degrees of diversity in dialect, social system, and other aspects of culture.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrye2004-1)

With numerous artistic, scientific, architectural, and philosophical achievements and numerous kingdoms and empires that bridged much of the civilized world in antiquity, the Iranian peoples were often in close contact with people from various western and eastern parts of the world.

### Religion

Main articles: [Ancient Iranian religion](/wiki/Ancient_Iranian_religion "Ancient Iranian religion") and [Iranian religions](/wiki/Iranian_religions "Iranian religions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Anahita_temple_Iran.jpg/250px-Anahita_temple_Iran.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anahita_temple_Iran.jpg)

The [ruins at Kangavar](/wiki/Temple_of_Anahita,_Kangavar "Temple of Anahita, Kangavar"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), presumed to belong to a temple dedicated to the ancient goddess [Anahita](/wiki/Anahita "Anahita").[[134]](#cite_note-138)

The early Iranian peoples practiced the [ancient Iranian religion](/wiki/Ancient_Iranian_religion "Ancient Iranian religion"), which, like [that of other Indo-European peoples](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_mythology "Proto-Indo-European mythology"), embraced various male and female deities.[[135]](#cite_note-Ancient_religion-139) Fire was regarded as an important and highly sacred element, and also [a deity](/wiki/Atar "Atar"). In ancient Iran, fire was kept with great care in [fire temples](/wiki/Fire_temple "Fire temple").[[135]](#cite_note-Ancient_religion-139) Various annual festivals that were mainly related to agriculture and herding were celebrated, the most important of which was the New Year (Nowruz), which is still widely celebrated.[[135]](#cite_note-Ancient_religion-139) [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), a form of the ancient Iranian religion that is still practiced by some communities,[[136]](#cite_note-140) was later developed and spread to nearly all of the Iranian peoples living in the Iranian Plateau. Other religions that had their origins in the Iranian world were [Mithraism](/wiki/Mithraism "Mithraism"), [Manichaeism](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism"), and [Mazdakism](/wiki/Mazdak "Mazdak"), among others. The various religions of the Iranian peoples are believed by some scholars to have been significant early philosophical influences on [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism").[[137]](#cite_note-141)

Nowadays, most Iranian people follow Islam (Sunnism, followed by Shi'ism), with minorities following Christianity, Judaism, [Mandaeism](/wiki/Mandaeism "Mandaeism"), Iranian religions and various levels of irreligion.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Cultural assimilation

See also: [Turco-Persian tradition](/wiki/Turco-Persian_tradition "Turco-Persian tradition"), [Persianate society](/wiki/Persianate_society "Persianate society"), and [Sarmatism](/wiki/Sarmatism "Sarmatism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/SurenaImage.jpg/250px-SurenaImage.jpg)](/wiki/File:SurenaImage.jpg)

[Bronze Statue of a Parthian nobleman](/wiki/Statue,_National_Museum_of_Iran_2401 "Statue, National Museum of Iran 2401"), National Museum of Iran

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Caftan_MET_DT1115.jpg/250px-Caftan_MET_DT1115.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caftan_MET_DT1115.jpg)

A [caftan](/wiki/Caftan "Caftan") worn by a Sogdian horseman, 8th–10th century

Iranian languages were and, to a lesser extent, still are spoken in a wide area comprising regions around the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), Russia and the [northwest of China](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang").[[138]](#cite_note-Iranica-CA-142) This population was linguistically assimilated by smaller but dominant Turkic-speaking groups, while the sedentary population eventually adopted the [Persian language](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), which began to spread within the region since the time of the Sasanian Empire.[[138]](#cite_note-Iranica-CA-142) The language-shift from Middle Iranian to Turkic and New Persian was predominantly the result of an "elite dominance" process.[[139]](#cite_note-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144) Moreover, various Turkic-speaking ethnic groups of the Iranian Plateau are often conversant also in an Iranian language and embrace Iranian culture to the extent that the term *[Turko-Iranian](/wiki/Turko-Iranian "Turko-Iranian")* would be applied.[[141]](#cite_note-145) A number of Iranian peoples were also intermixed with the [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"),[[9]](#cite_note-Taylor_&_Francis-9) and many were subjected to [Slavicisation](/wiki/Slavicisation "Slavicisation").[[10]](#cite_note-Women_in_Russia-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Slovene_Studies-11)

The following either partially descend from or are sometimes regarded as descendants of the Iranian peoples.

* [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages")-speakers:

See also: [Old Azeri language](/wiki/Old_Azeri_language "Old Azeri language") and [Origin of the Azerbaijanis](/wiki/Origin_of_the_Azerbaijanis "Origin of the Azerbaijanis")

* + [Azerbaijanis](/wiki/Azerbaijanis "Azerbaijanis"): In spite of being native speakers of a Turkic language ([Azerbaijani Turkic](/wiki/Azerbaijani_language "Azerbaijani language")), they are believed to be primarily descended from the earlier Iranian-speakers of the region.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrye2004-1)[[131]](#cite_note-LANDS_OF_IRAN-135)[[142]](#cite_note-146)[[143]](#cite_note-roy-147)[[144]](#cite_note-148) They are possibly related to the ancient Iranian tribe of the [Medes](/wiki/Medes "Medes"), aside from the rise of the subsequent [Persian](/wiki/Persianization "Persianization") and [Turkic elements](/wiki/Turkification "Turkification") (changing of the native Iranian language) within their area of settlement,[[145]](#cite_note-149) which, prior to the spread of Turkic, was Iranian-speaking.[[146]](#cite_note-150) Thus, due to their historical, genetic and cultural ties to the Iranians,[[147]](#cite_note-encyclopedia1-151) the Azerbaijanis are often associated with the Iranian peoples. Genetic studies observed that they are also genetically related to the Iranian peoples.[[148]](#cite_note-152)
  + [Turkmens](/wiki/Turkmens "Turkmens"): Genetic studies show that the Turkmens are characterized by the presence of local Iranian mtDNA lineages, similar to the eastern Iranian populations, but modest female [Mongoloid](/wiki/Mongoloid "Mongoloid") [mtDNA](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_DNA_haplogroup "Human mitochondrial DNA haplogroup") components were observed in Turkmen populations with the frequencies of about 20%.[[149]](#cite_note-153)
  + [Uzbeks](/wiki/Uzbeks "Uzbeks"): The unique grammatical and phonetical features of the [Uzbek language](/wiki/Uzbek_language "Uzbek language"),[[150]](#cite_note-154) as well as elements within the modern Uzbek culture, reflect the older Iranian roots of the Uzbek people.[[138]](#cite_note-Iranica-CA-142)[[151]](#cite_note-155)[[152]](#cite_note-156)[[153]](#cite_note-157) According to recent genetic genealogy testing from a University of Oxford study, the genetic admixture of the Uzbeks clusters somewhere between the Iranian peoples and the Mongols.[[154]](#cite_note-158) Prior to the [Russian conquest of Central Asia](/wiki/Russian_conquest_of_Central_Asia "Russian conquest of Central Asia"), the local ancestors of the Turkic-speaking Uzbeks and the Persian-speaking Tajiks, both living in Central Asia, were referred to as *[Sarts](/wiki/Sart "Sart")*, while *Uzbek* and *Turk* were the names given to the nomadic and semi-nomadic populations of the area. Still, as of today, modern Uzbeks and Tajiks are known to their Turkic neighbors, the [Kazakhs](/wiki/Kazakhs "Kazakhs") and the [Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_people "Kyrgyz people"), as *Sarts*. Some Uzbek scholars also favor the Iranian origin theory.[[155]](#cite_note-zbekiston1994-159)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] However, another study, conducted in 2009, claims that Uzbeks and Central Asian Turkic peoples cluster genetically and are far from Iranian groups.[[156]](#cite_note-160)
  + [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs"): Contemporary scholars consider modern Uyghurs to be the descendants of, apart from the ancient Uyghurs, the Iranian [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka") ([Scythian](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians")) tribes and other Indo-European peoples who inhabited the [Tarim Basin](/wiki/Tarim_Basin "Tarim Basin") before the arrival of the Turkic tribes.[[157]](#cite_note-xinjiang-161)
* [Persian](/wiki/Persian_languages "Persian languages")-speakers:
  + The [Hazaras](/wiki/Hazaras "Hazaras") are a Persian-speaking ethnic group native to, and primarily residing in, the mountainous region of [Hazarajat](/wiki/Hazarajat "Hazarajat"), in central Afghanistan. Although the origins of the Hazara people have not been fully reconstructed, genetic analysis of the Hazara indicate partial [Mongol](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") ancestry. Mongol and Turkic invaders ([Turco-Mongols](/wiki/Turco-Mongol_tradition "Turco-Mongol tradition")) mixed with the local indigenous Turkic and Iranian populations; for example, [Qara'unas](/wiki/Qara%27unas "Qara'unas") settled in what is now Afghanistan and mixed with the local populations. A second wave of mostly [Chagatai](/wiki/Chagatai_Khanate "Chagatai Khanate") Turco-Mongols came from Central Asia, associated with the [Ilkhanate](/wiki/Ilkhanate "Ilkhanate") and the [Timurids](/wiki/Timurid_Empire "Timurid Empire"), all of whom settled in Hazarajat and mixed with the local populations. Phenotype can vary, with some noting that certain Hazaras may resemble peoples native to the Iranian plateau.[[158]](#cite_note-162)[[159]](#cite_note-163)
* [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages")-speakers:
  + [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats") and [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs"): Some scholars suggest that the Slavic-speaking Serbs and Croats are descended from the ancient [Sarmatians](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians"),[[160]](#cite_note-164)[[161]](#cite_note-165) an ancient Iranian people who once settled in most of southern European Russia and the eastern [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), and that their ethnonyms are of Iranian origin. It is proposed that the Sarmatian *[Serboi](/wiki/Serboi "Serboi")* and alleged *Horoathos* tribes were assimilated with the numerically superior Slavs, passing on their name. Iranian-speaking peoples did inhabit parts of the Balkans in late classical times, and would have been encountered by the Slavs. An archaeogenetic IBD study found that the Slavs make a specific and recognisable genetic cluster which "was formed by admixture of a Baltic-related group with East Germanic people and [Sarmatians](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians") or [Scythians](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians")".[[162]](#cite_note-166) Although previous direct linguistic, historical, or archaeological proof for such a theory is lacking.[[e]](#cite_note-167)
* [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language")-speakers:
  + [Shirazis](/wiki/Shirazi_people "Shirazi people"): The Shirazi are a sub-group of the [Swahili people](/wiki/Swahili_people "Swahili people") living on the [Swahili coast](/wiki/Swahili_coast "Swahili coast") of [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"), especially on the islands of [Zanzibar](/wiki/Zanzibar "Zanzibar"), [Pemba](/wiki/Pemba_Island "Pemba Island"), and [Comoros](/wiki/Comoros "Comoros"). Local traditions about their origin claim they are descended from merchant princes from [Shiraz](/wiki/Shiraz "Shiraz") in Iran who settled along the Swahili coast.
* [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") speakers:
  + [Sindhis](/wiki/Sindhis "Sindhis"): About 40% of Pakistan's Baloch population lives in [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), many of whom speak [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language").[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbdul_Rasheed2017-168)[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarrison2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidXfI-hEI8a9wCpgPA28_28]-169) It is believed that the first Baloch came to Sindh during the [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age"), with further waves of migration during the 18th century. The [Talpur Dynasty](/wiki/Talpur_Dynasty "Talpur Dynasty") was an ethnic [Baloch](/wiki/Talpur "Talpur") dynasty that ruled much of Sindh and parts of [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan "Balochistan") during the [British colonial period](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmed199844-170) The industrialisation of [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") under [direct British rule in Sindh](/wiki/Sind_Division "Sind Division") drew further migrants from Balochistan, including Baloch fleeing [Qajar](/wiki/Qajar_Iran "Qajar Iran") and [Pahlavi Iran](/wiki/Pahlavi_Iran "Pahlavi Iran") and Afro-Baloch [Makranis](/wiki/Siddi#Pakistan "Siddi").[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaher2014-171)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGayer2014127–129-172) The [Baloch in Sindh](/wiki/Baloch_people_in_Sindh "Baloch people in Sindh") are known as the *Baruch* (ٻروچ).[[168]](#cite_note-173)

## Genetics

Further information: [Genetic history of the Middle East](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_Middle_East "Genetic history of the Middle East")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Genetic_distance_of_worldwide_populations.png/250px-Genetic_distance_of_worldwide_populations.png)](/wiki/File:Genetic_distance_of_worldwide_populations.png)

Population genomic PCA, showing the CIC (Central Iranian cluster) among other worldwide samples.

Recent population genomic studies found that the genetic structure of Iranian peoples formed already about 5,000 years ago and show high continuity since then, suggesting that they were largely unaffected by migration events from outside groups. Genetically speaking, Iranian peoples generally cluster closely with [European](/wiki/European_peoples "European peoples") and other [Middle Eastern peoples](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_peoples "Middle Eastern peoples"). Analyzed samples of Iranian [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"), [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds"), [Azeris](/wiki/Azeris "Azeris"), [Lurs](/wiki/Lurs "Lurs"), [Mazanderanis](/wiki/Mazanderanis "Mazanderanis"), [Gilaks](/wiki/Gilaks "Gilaks") and [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") cluster tightly together, forming a single cluster known as the CIC (Central Iranian cluster). Compared with worldwide populations, Iranians (CIC) cluster in the center of the wider West-Eurasian cluster, close to Europeans, Middle Easterners, and South-Central Asians. Iranian Arabs and Azeris genetically overlap with Iranian peoples. The genetic substructure of Iranians is low and homogeneous, compared with other "1000G" populations. Europeans, and certain South Asians (specifically the Parsi minority) showed the highest affinity with Iranians, while Sub-Saharan Africans and East Asians showed the highest differentiation with Iranians.[[169]](#cite_note-174)

The [BMAC](/wiki/Bactria%E2%80%93Margiana_Archaeological_Complex "Bactria–Margiana Archaeological Complex") population largely derived from preceding local [Copper Age](/wiki/Copper_Age "Copper Age") peoples who were in turn related to [Neolithic farmers](/wiki/Iranian_hunter-gatherers "Iranian hunter-gatherers") from the [Iranian plateau](/wiki/Iranian_plateau "Iranian plateau") and to a lesser extent early [Anatolian farmers](/wiki/Anatolian_hunter-gatherers "Anatolian hunter-gatherers"), as well as [West Siberian hunter-gatherers](/wiki/West_Siberian_hunter-gatherer "West Siberian hunter-gatherer"). The samples extracted from the BMAC sites did not have derived any part of their ancestry from the [Yamnaya](/wiki/Yamnaya "Yamnaya") people, who are associated with [Proto-Indo-Europeans](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans"), although some peripheral samples did already carry significant Yamnaya-like [Western Steppe Herders](/wiki/Western_Steppe_Herders "Western Steppe Herders") ancestry, inline with the southwards expansion of Western Steppe Herders from the Sintashta and Andronovo cultures towards Southern Central Asia at c. 2100 BCE.[[170]](#cite_note-175)

The [Yaz I culture](/wiki/Yaz_culture "Yaz culture") of Margiana, Bactria and Sogdia was characterised by a combination of BMAC and Andronovo ancestries.[[58]](#cite_note-Yaz_culture-59) Likewise, a 2022 study also shows that the ancestry of modern [Tajiks](/wiki/Tajiks "Tajiks") and [Yaghnobis](/wiki/Yaghnobis "Yaghnobis") largely formed during the early Iron Age by a mixture between these two groups.[[171]](#cite_note-176)

### Paternal haplogroups

Regueiro *et al* (2006)[[172]](#cite_note-177) and Grugni *et al* (2012)[[173]](#cite_note-Grugni_et_al_2012-178) have performed large-scale sampling of Y chromosome haplogroups of different ethnic groups *within Iran*. They found that the most common paternal haplogroups were:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Kurdish_people_celebrating_Nowruz_2018%2C_Tangi_Sar_village_%2813970105000310636575781018607613_30731%29.jpg/250px-Kurdish_people_celebrating_Nowruz_2018%2C_Tangi_Sar_village_%2813970105000310636575781018607613_30731%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kurdish_people_celebrating_Nowruz_2018,_Tangi_Sar_village_(13970105000310636575781018607613_30731).jpg)

Kurdish people celebrating [Nowruz](/wiki/Nowruz "Nowruz"), [Tangi Sar](/wiki/Tangi_Sar "Tangi Sar") village.

* **[J1](/wiki/Haplogroup_J1 "Haplogroup J1")-M267**; commonly found among [Semitic-speaking](/wiki/Semitic_language "Semitic language") people, was rarely over 10% in Iranian groups.
* **[J2](/wiki/Haplogroup_J2 "Haplogroup J2")-M172**: is the most common Hg in Iran (~23%); almost exclusively represented by J2a-M410 subclade (93%), the other major sub-clade being J2b-M12. Apart from Iranians, J2 is common in northern Arabs, Mediterranean and Balkan peoples (Croats, Serbs, Greeks, Bosniaks, Albanians, Italians, Macedonians, Bulgarians, Turks), in the Caucasus (Armenians, Georgians, Chechens, Ingush, northeastern Turkey, north/northwestern Iran, Kurds, Persians); whilst its frequency drops suddenly beyond Afghanistan, Pakistan and northern India.[[174]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-179) In Europe, J2a is more common in southern Greece and southern Italy; whilst J2b (J2-M12) is more common in Thessaly, Macedonia and central – northern Italy. Thus J2a and its subgroups within it have a wide distribution from Italy to India, whilst J2b is mostly confined to the Balkans and Italy,[[175]](#cite_note-180) being rare even in Turkey. Whilst closely linked with Anatolia and the Levant; and putative agricultural expansions, the distribution of the various sub-clades of J2 likely represents a number of migrational histories which require further elucidation.[[174]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-179)[[176]](#cite_note-181)
* **[R1a](/wiki/Haplogroup_R1a "Haplogroup R1a")-M198**: is common in Iran, more so in the east and south rather than the west and north; suggesting a migration toward the south to India then a secondary westward spread across Iran.[[177]](#cite_note-182) Whilst the Grongi and Regueiro studies did not define exactly which sub-clades Iranian R1a haplogrouops belong to, private genealogy tests suggest that they virtually all belong to "Eurasian" R1a-Z93.[[178]](#cite_note-183) Indeed, population studies of neighbouring Indian groups found that they all were in R1a-Z93.[[179]](#cite_note-Pamjav_et_al_2012-184) This implies that R1a in Iran did not descend from "European" R1a, or vice versa. Rather, both groups are collateral, brother branches which descend from a parental group hypothesized to have initially lived somewhere between central Asia and Eastern Europe.[[179]](#cite_note-Pamjav_et_al_2012-184)
* **[R1b](/wiki/Haplogroup_R1b "Haplogroup R1b") – M269**: is widespread from Ireland to Iran, and is common in highland West Asian populations such as Armenians, Turks and Iranians – with an average frequency of 8.5%. Iranian R1b belongs to the L-23 subclade,[[180]](#cite_note-185) which is an older than the derivative subclade (R1b-M412) which is most common in western Europe.[[181]](#cite_note-186)
* [Haplogroup **G**](/wiki/Haplogroup_G_(Y-DNA) "Haplogroup G (Y-DNA)") and subclades: most concentrated in the Caucasus,[[182]](#cite_note-187) it is present in 10% of Iranians.[[173]](#cite_note-Grugni_et_al_2012-178)
* [Haplogroup **E**](/wiki/Haplogroup_E-M96 "Haplogroup E-M96") and various subclades are frequently found among Middle Easterners, Europeans, northern and eastern African populations. They are present in less than 10% of Iranians.

Two large – scale papers by Haber (2012)[[183]](#cite_note-Haber_et_al_2012-188) and Di Cristofaro (2013)[[184]](#cite_note-Di_Cristofaro_et_al_2013-189) analyzed populations from Afghanistan, where several Iranian-speaking groups are native. They found that different groups (e.g. Baluch, Hazara, Pashtun) were quite diverse, yet overall:

* **R1a** (subclade not further analyzed) was the predominant haplogroup, especially amongst Pashtuns, the Baloch and Tajiks.
* The presence of "East-Eurasian" haplogroup [**C3**](/wiki/Haplogroup_C3 "Haplogroup C3"), especially in Hazaras (33–40%), in part linked to Mongol expansions into the region.
* The presence of haplogroup J2, like in Iran, of 5–20%.
* A relative paucity of "Indian" [haplogroup **H**](/wiki/Haplogroup_H_(Y-DNA) "Haplogroup H (Y-DNA)") (< 10%).

A 2012 study by Grugni et al. analyzed the haplogroups of 15 different ethnic groups from Iran. They found that about 31.4% belong to J, 29.1% belong to R, 11.8% belong to G, and 9.2% belong to E. They found that Iranian ethnic groups display high haplogroup diversity, compared to other Middle Easterners. The authors concluded that the Iranian gene pool has been an important source for the Middle Eastern and Eurasian Y chromosome diversity, and the results suggest that there was already rather high Y chromosome diversity during the Neolithic period, placing Iranian populations in between Europeans, Middle Easterners and South Asians.[[185]](#cite_note-190)

A 2024 study by Vallini et al. stated that ancient and modern populations in the Iranian plateau have a similar genetic component to the Ancient West Eurasian lineage which stayed in the 'population hub' (WEC2). But they also display some ancestry from [Basal Eurasians](/wiki/Basal_Eurasian "Basal Eurasian") and [Ancient East Eurasians](/wiki/Ancient_East_Eurasians "Ancient East Eurasians") via contact events starting in the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic").[[186]](#cite_note-191)

## Explanatory notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** In the Avesta the *airiia-* are members of the ethnic group of the Avesta-reciters themselves, in contradistinction to the *anairiia-*, the "[non-Aryas](/wiki/Aniran "Aniran")". The word also appears four times in Old Persian: One is in the [Behistun inscription](/wiki/Behistun_inscription "Behistun inscription"), where *ariya-* is the name of a language or script (DB 4.89). The other three instances occur in [Darius I](/wiki/Darius_I "Darius I")'s inscription at [Naqsh-e Rustam](/wiki/Naqsh-e_Rustam "Naqsh-e Rustam") (DNa 14–15), in Darius I's inscription at Susa (DSe 13–14), and in the inscription of [Xerxes I](/wiki/Xerxes_I "Xerxes I") at [Persepolis](/wiki/Persepolis "Persepolis") (XPh 12–13). In these, the two Achaemenid dynasts describe themselves as *pārsa pārsahyā puça ariya ariyaciça* "a Persian, son of a Persian, an Ariya, of Ariya origin". "The phrase with *ciça*, "origin, descendance", assures that it [i.e. *ariya*] is an ethnic name wider in meaning than *pārsa* and not a simple adjectival epithet".[[25]](#cite_note-Bailey_Arya-25)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** [Apollonius](/wiki/Apollonius_of_Rhodes "Apollonius of Rhodes") (*[Argonautica](/wiki/Argonautica "Argonautica")*, iii) envisaged the *Sauromatai* as the bitter foe of King [Aietes](/wiki/Aietes "Aietes") of [Colchis](/wiki/Colchis "Colchis") (modern Georgia).
3. **[^](#cite_ref-102)** Western and Eastern Iranics are determined based on their proximity to [Greater Iran](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran") and their linguistic and cultural characteristics. Western Iranic peoples, such as Achomi Persians, Fars, Kurds, Lors, and Talysh, historically inhabited regions closer to the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau") and speak languages belonging to the Southwestern and Northwestern branches of the Iranic family. Eastern Iranic peoples, including Pashtuns, Baloch, Sogdians, and others, traditionally lived in areas extending toward Central and South Asia, speaking languages that evolved from the Eastern branch of Old Iranian. This division reflects both geographic settlement patterns and deep linguistic evolution over millennia.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-113)** There is a conflict on their classification

"Seven groups (Iranian Arabs, Azeris, Gilaks, Kurds, Mazanderanis, Lurs and Persians) strongly overlapped in their overall autosomal diversity in an MDS analysis (Fig 1B), suggesting the existence of a Central Iranian Cluster (CIC), notably also including Iranian Arabs and Azeris. On a global scale (Fig 2 including "Old World" populations only; see S2 Fig for all 1000G populations), CIC Iranians closely clustered with Europeans, while Iranian Turkmen showed similar yet distinct degrees of admixture compared to other South Asians. A local comparison corroborated the distinct genetic diversity of CIC Iranians relative to other geographically close populations [2, 6, 44] (Fig 3 and S3 Fig). Still, genetic substructure was much smaller among Iranian groups than in relation to any of the 1000G populations, supporting the view that the CIC groups form a distinct genetic entity, despite internal heterogeneity. European (FST~0.0105–0.0294), South Asians (FST~0.0141–0.0338), but also some Latin American populations (Puerto Ricans: FST~0.0153–0.0228; Colombians: FST~0.0170–0.0261) were closest to Iranians, whereas Sub-Saharan Africans and admixed Afro-Americans (FST~0.0764–0.1424) as well as East Asians (FST ~ 0.0645–0.1055) showed large degrees of differentiation with Iranians."
